title: Riving an ash bowl blank
subtitle: Splitting a log section along the grain to produce a stable carving blank
excerpt: A rived bowl blank is stronger than a sawn one and less likely to crack during carving or drying, because the grain runs unbroken across the bowl wall. This technique covers reading end-grain for riving direction, splitting a log section in progressive halves, selecting the blank with the best grain for bowl work, and trimming with a side axe.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for riving method and blank selection; traditional European spoon-carving practice for radial-rive bowl geometry.

content.text: A bowl blank from a sawn log section includes pith and growth rings running across the bowl walls; the two main causes of checking and cracking as the wood dries. A rived blank, split radially from the log, places the growth rings running perpendicular to the bowl wall so moisture escapes evenly and the blank stays stable. The technique also produces a blank whose grain runs unbroken around the bowl curves, which is why rived blanks survive the carving process better than sawn ones.
Green wood
ash is the standard British bowl blank material; it rives predictably and carves well at high moisture content.
Reading the end grain
Before the first
froe

stroke, read the end grain of the log section. The growth rings should run in concentric circles around the pith. Any twist in these rings; visible as a spiral pattern on the bark; indicates spiral grain that will make
riving

unpredictable and the finished blank liable to twist as it dries. Do not use spiral-grained sections for bowl blanks. Identify the pith (the dark centre dot) and the medullary rays; the fine radial lines that run outward from the pith. The froe blade goes between two adjacent medullary rays for the cleanest split.
Step-by-step

Stand the log section on a low chopping block, end grain up. Mark a halving line across the end grain through the pith. Set the froe blade on the mark, centred on the pith, and strike the froe spine with the beetle mallet five to six times to drive the blade 5 cm into the wood. Lever the froe handle to start the split, then drive further and lever again in 5 cm increments until the section separates into two halves.

Remove the pith from one half by riving again; set the froe just to one side of the pith and split the pith strip free. The pith is the most unstable part of the wood; a blank that includes the pith will always crack through it during drying regardless of species. The finished blank should have no pith remaining.

If the log section is large enough, rive the half into quarters radially. Place the froe at 90 degrees to the halving split and rive again. Each quarter is a potential bowl blank; the grain runs radially outward from the pith in all four. Select the quarter with the fewest knots and the most even grain spacing; this will produce the most stable finished bowl.

Trim the blank to bowl shape with the side axe. A basic bowl blank is a wedge-shaped piece with the wide face where the bowl will be carved and the narrow back face roughly parallel to it. Remove the worst of the surplus wood; the deep corners of the quarter section; with axe cuts along the grain, leaving 15 mm more than the intended bowl wall thickness on all sides.

Carve the blank immediately after riving, while the wood is at maximum moisture content. A freshly rived ash blank carves in half the time of one that has been sitting for 3 days. If carving must wait, wrap the blank in a plastic bag to slow moisture loss.
What a good blank looks like

A correctly rived bowl blank has growth rings running at 90 degrees to the widest face; when you look at the back of the blank, the growth rings should appear as straight parallel lines running across the full width, not as curves or arcs. This radial orientation is the marker of a correctly riven blank; sawn blanks have curved rings on the back face. The side-axe trimmed blank should have no flat sawn face; all surfaces should show the characteristic sheen of a riven split rather than the dull, fibrous texture of a sawn cut.
Common mistakes
Leaving the pith in the blank: the pith always cracks during drying. Remove it in the first riving step, even if it means a slightly thinner blank.
Choosing spiral-grained wood: the split will run off-line and the finished blank will not be usable. Check the end grain and bark pattern for spiral before starting.
Leaving the blank unworked for more than 3 days: end checks develop quickly in warm weather and destabilise the blank. Work it immediately or keep it sealed in a bag until ready.
